title: Eager for a break, farmers don't expect much relief after the Iran peace deal
summary:
Eager for a break, farmers don't expect much relief after the Iran peace dealtoggle caption Kirk Siegler/NPRFertilizer prices are finally falling this week after news of a tentative peace deal with Iran and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.
Farmers are equally eager for relief from high diesel prices.
But an updated U.S. Department of Agriculture commodities forecast last Thursday predicts energy and fertilizer prices won't go down substantially until 2027, putting the heartland in a further bind.
Many farmers in the Midwest were able to lock in their fertilizer prices before the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, in some cases due to lucky timing.
Iran peace deal or not, so many farmers in the Heartland were already going into 2026 on the financial brink.

title: Israel and Lebanon reach framework agreement
summary:
Israel and Hezbollah have traded cross-border fire, with both accusing each other of violating the agreement, but the intensity has dropped off in recent days.
But shortly after the signing, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu reiterated that Israeli forces would remain in southern Lebanon until Hezbollah disarms.
While Trump has insisted that Israel has a right to defend itself from Hezbollah rocket strikes on its territories, he has also claimed he can "control Israel from attacking Lebanon".
Lebanon was drawn into the war between the US and Israel against Iran on 2 March, when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel in retaliation for an Israeli strike that killed Iran's supreme leader.
A US-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon on 16 April failed to stop the fighting.

title: UAE stresses Hormuz security in rare call with Iran after war tensions
summary:
The exchange, reported by state news agency WAM, marks a rare public contact between Abu Dhabi and Tehran following tensions linked to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
It was the first ‌announced conversation between the ⁠two ministers since the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, which were followed by Iranian ⁠attacks across the Gulf, including in the United Arab Emirates, where the U.S. has military bases.
He also highlighted respect for sovereignty, adherence to international law and the uninterrupted flow of maritime traffic through Hormuz.
Sheikh Abdullah ​said diplomacy remained the best way to resolve crises, expressing hope that ongoing efforts would lead to ​lasting security and ‌stability in the region, WAM reported.
(Reporting by Muhammad Al ​Gebaly and Enas Alashray in Cairo; Editing by ​Alison Williams and Ros Russell)

title: Ukraine is spilling its enemies' military secrets. Soldiers, arms makers, and foreign governments all want access.
summary:
Ukrainian soldiers, defense companies, and foreign governments are rushing to sign up for a new portal where Kyiv is offering access to captured Russian military hardware — and what's inside.
This portal contains technical data from Russian weapons like drones, missiles, and armored vehicles, which approved users can access in a first-of-its-kind information-sharing arrangement between Ukraine and its Western partners.
"We are obliged to share this information with the Ukrainian defense manufacturers, Myronenko said.
Ukraine's partners benefit from the information on Russian weapons, and Kyiv benefits from their research.
Myronenko said that these include Ukrainian defense companies and military units, as well as foreign companies and governments.

title: Pragmatic choice: Israel’s war backfires as Gulf backs US-Iran deal
summary:
Doha, Qatar – Gulf states have welcomed a breakthrough agreement between the United States and Iran to end a war they never wanted.
But in a twist of irony, Israeli aggression in this pursuit has pushed some Gulf states closer to Tehran.
But this is shaped more by the Gulf states wanting the war to end rather than a newfound trust of Iran.
Gulf states will want a more comprehensive agreement between Iran and the US, rather than the nuclear-focused JCPOA, said Pinfold.
“They are at the heart of the negotiations,” Haghirian said regarding the Gulf states’ role in the current talks.

title: UN nuclear chief says inspectors will visit Iran sites as part of war deal
summary:
In recent days, there has been a dispute between the US and Iran over the issue of UN nuclear inspectors visiting sites in the country.
On Monday, following talks in Switzerland with Iran's chief negotiator, US Vice-President JD Vance said Iran had "agreed to invite IAEA inspectors back into their country".
Enriched uranium can be used to make reactor fuel but also nuclear weapons.
Iran insists its nuclear activities are entirely peaceful and that it would never seek to develop or acquire nuclear weapons.
Under a 2015 deal with the US and five other world powers, Iran agreed to limit its nuclear activities and allow continuous and robust monitoring by the IAEA's inspectors in return for relief from crippling economic sanctions.

title: UN agency working to restart Hormuz evacuations after ship attack
summary:
The U.N.'s International Maritime Organization said on Thursday it had "temporarily paused" its evacuation ​initiative after a container ship operated by Taiwan's Evergreen was attacked.
Advertisement AdvertisementSome 115 vessels and around 2,500 seafarers were able to sail through the strait before evacuations were paused, IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez told a virtual news conference.
He added that the IMO was investigating "the reasons and motivation" for the ship attack.
Advertisement AdvertisementThe so-called ​Traffic Separation Scheme, adopted by the IMO in 1968, established routing lanes ‌through Iranian and Omani waters in the ​strait.
"The quicker we can resume operations, of course, ​the quicker we can start increasing the numbers until we get that evacuation ​completed."

title: South Africa rejects US pressure over foreign policy stance
summary:
Ramaphosa’s remarks come after US Ambassador to South Africa Leo Brent Bozell III criticized South Africa’s non-aligned foreign policy stance on X, referencing its engagements with China and Iran, including Deputy President Paul Mashatile’s visit to Beijing.
He said the country engages globally on the basis of sovereign equality, mutual respect and non-interference, adding that South Africa does not regard itself as having enemies.
He said South Africa values its long-standing relationship with the United States and continues cooperation in areas such as trade, investment, health, education and security.
He also stressed South Africa’s non-aligned stance allows it to engage widely on trade disputes, including tariff measures imposed by major economies.
Responding to criticism that South Africa is not consistent in its approach to authoritarian states, Ramaphosa said foreign policy is guided by constitutional values but applied using different diplomatic instruments depending on context.
